sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5128 as the nearest easily observable elliptical galaxy and an ideal target for studying unusually bright globular clusters in a populous host system.

quotes.quote: In order to investigate whether the brightest globular clusters in the giant elliptical galaxies are similar to the less luminous globular clusters like those found in Local Group galaxies, we study the velocity dispersion and structural parameter correlations of a sample of bright globular clusters in the nearest giant elliptical galaxy NGC 5128 (Centaurus A). UVES echelle spectrograph on the ESO Very Large Telescope (VLT) was used to obtain high resolution spectra of 23 bright globular clusters in NGC 5128, and 10 clusters were observed with EMMI in echelle mode with the ESO New Technology Telescope.

The nearest easily observable elliptical galaxy is NGC 5128. It has a large number of bright globular clusters with luminosities exceeding the brightest Local Group globulars. This makes it an ideal target.

sources.summary: The paper reports mid-infrared and submillimeter observations of Centaurus A, finding a bisymmetric barred-spiral dust structure around its AGN formed from accreted gas-rich tidal debris.

quotes.quote: We report observations at mid-infrared and sub-millimeter wavelengths of Centaurus A (CenA, NGC 5128), the giant elliptical galaxy that harbors the closest radio loud Active Galactic Nucleus (AGN) to Earth. The dust emission from the deep interior of CenA reveals a bisymmetric structure with a diameter of 5 ^ (5 kpc), centred at the AGN. This structure is remarkably similar to that of a barred spiral galaxy, with the bar lying in a plane that is tilted 18 ^ from the line of sight. The true nature of the distribution of dust in the inner regions of CenA is noticeably displaced from the more chaotic and widespread optical obscuration. The barred spiral is a quasi-stable structure formed at the center of the giant elliptical from the tidal debris of a gas-rich object(s) accreted in the past 10 ^9 years.

sources.summary: The paper describes Centaurus A as a nearby faint accreting supermassive-black-hole system with dust bands, a compact central mass, and energetic particle beams.

quotes.quote: An archetype of this group, Centaurus A, graces the southern constellation of Centaurus at a distance of 11 million light-years (see figure 5). At the center of the dark bands of dust, HST recently uncovered a disk of glowing, high-speed gas, swirling about a concentration of matter with the mass of 2 10^6 M_ . This enormous mass within the central cavity cannot be due to normal stars, since these objects would shine brightly, producing an intense optical spike toward the middle, unlike the rather tempered look of the infrared image shown here.  Centaurus A is apparently funneling highly energetic particles into beams perpendicular to the dark strands of dust.

sources.summary: Deep Chandra data resolve the Centaurus A jet, showing shock-dominated inner knots, distributed particle acceleration farther out, and extended counterjet emission.

quotes.quote: We present new deep Chandra observations of the Centaurus A jet, with a combined on-source exposure time of 719 ks. These data allow detailed X-ray spectral measurements to be made along the jet out to its disappearance at 4.5 kpc from the nucleus. We distinguish several regimes of high-energy particle acceleration: while the inner part of the jet is dominated by knots and has properties consistent with local particle acceleration at shocks, the particle acceleration in the outer 3.4 kpc of the jet is likely to be dominated by an unknown distributed acceleration mechanism. In addition to several compact counterjet features we detect probable extended emission from a counterjet out to 2.0 kpc from the nucleus, and argue that this implies that the diffuse acceleration process operates in the counterjet as well.

sources.summary: The paper includes Cen A as a multi-epoch Chandra target, using six 100 ks ACIS-I observations plus archival data and reporting two clear luminous transients with possibly anomalous rates.

quotes.quote: Recently six 100 ks ACIS-I observations of Cen A 3.7 Mpc , averaging 5 distance indicators from 6 in GRS_FMS+2007 were analyzed with shorter archival observations taken 4 - 7 yr earlier.

In the more nearby Cen A, there are two clear, luminous transients, but no luminous persistent sources GRS_SKJ+2007 . One source is a recurring transient with outbursts that lasts 2 yr , while the single outburst of the other has lasted > 70 d . Unless the latter source proves to have a much longer duration outburst, the rates and types of luminous transients in Cen A may be anomalous compared to other early-type galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents Centaurus A as the nearest radio and optically luminous early-type galaxy, observed repeatedly by Chandra, with an anomalously high luminous-transient rate if the reported outburst is not long-lived.

quotes.quote: Centaurus A (NGC 5128, Cen A), at 3.7 Mpc the average of 5 distance indicators, see 6 in FMS+2007 , is the nearest radio galaxy and the nearest optically luminous M_B = -21.1 ; DHM+1979 early-type galaxy. Prior to 2007, Chandra had targeted Cen A with the ACIS detectors four times, 1999 December 5, 2000 May 17, 2002 September 3, and 2003 September 14 (Observations ADS/Sa.CXO#obs/00316 316 , ADS/Sa.CXO#obs/00962 962 , ADS/Sa.CXO#obs/02978 2978 , and ADS/Sa.CXO#obs/03965 3965 ). In 2007, Chandra performed six further deep observations ( 100 ks each) of Cen A to study the X-ray properties of its jet, lobes, and XRBs HKS+2007,JSM+2007,WBK+2007,KHS+2008s .
Unless this outburst lasts many years, the rate of luminous transients in Cen A is anomalously high compared to other early-type galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5128 as Centaurus A, the closest active radio galaxy, and finds unresolved central cores at 11.9 and 8.6 microns with weak extended emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 5128 Figure N5128 Centaurus A, the closest active radio galaxy. The 11.9 image shows an unresolved central core, with extended emission at 10 level. The 8.6 image shows an unresolved core. For comparison, see for a 10.4 image showing an unresolved core of less than 0.5'' .

sources.summary: The paper treats Centaurus A as the nearest radio-loud AGN and a possible HECR source whose distance permits iron nuclei to survive propagation, with acceleration to 10^20 eV considered plausible under the Hillas criterion.

quotes.quote: A significant fraction of the PAO events has arrival directions directed to Centaurus A (Cen A), the nearest radio-loud AGN ( 3.4 Mpc). Since the distance of Cen A is less than the mean free path of photo-disintegration of irons at 10^ 20 eV, irons can arrive at the Earth from this source without significant photo-disintegration. Also, irons can be most easily accelerated up to 10^ 20 eV following the Hillas criterion hillas84 . Although pure iron composition is an extreme case, a significant fraction of irons can be expected if Cen A is really one of the HECR sources.

sources.summary: The paper treats Centaurus A as an AGN near a cosmic-ray hot spot, motivates possible EeV neutrinos from it, includes it in the southern IceCube search, and compares its flux limit with predictions.

quotes.quote: Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) are possible source candidates and results from the Pierre Auger Observatory highlight, in particular, Centaurus A as an AGN near a cosmic ray hot spot in the southern hemisphere. Neutrinos up to EeV energies could be produced in hadronic acceleration processes at this site, predicted for example in .

With the extension to the southern hemisphere, this is the first IceCube analysis that allows a search for neutrinos from Centaurus A and the center of our galaxy, Sgr A ^* , and both were added to the list.
The flux limit obtained for Centaurus A is shown in Fig. fig_lim in relation to neutrino predictions from Cuoco Hannestad and Kachelriess et al.

sources.summary: The paper argues that UHECR clustering around Cen A and the Virgo absence support light-nuclei cosmic rays, with Cen A event spread shaped by Lorentz bending.

quotes.quote: The rise of nucleon UHECR above GZK astronomy made by protons (AUGER November 2007) is puzzled by three main mysteries: an unexpected nearby Virgo UHECR suppression (or absence), a rich crowded clustering frozen vertically along Cen A, a composition suggesting nuclei (not much directional) and not nucleons.
The He^4 fragility and its mass and charge explains the Virgo absence (due to He^4 opacity above few Mpc) and the Cen A spread clustering (a quarter of the whole sample).

However the peculiar spread of the events around Cen A makes us believe that vertical spread is not only related to galaxies distribution but also on Lorentz bending. The next ( figure fig4 ) describe the UHECR over the 408 Mhz cosmic background, showing the peak role of the Cen A emission and Vela emission with UHECR clustering.

sources.summary: The paper recovers two UHECRs previously associated with Centaurus A and argues that this nearby radio galaxy remains important for testing UHECR acceleration.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, we do recover the two UHECRs previously associated with the radio galaxy Centaurus A abraham5 and one UHECR consistent with the position of the starburst/Seyfert 2 NGC 4945 moskalenko . Both Centaurus A (1FGL J1325.6 4300) and NGC 4945 (1FGL J1305.4 4928) are detected by .

On the other hand, radio galaxies such as Centaurus A could potentially account for the acceleration of some UHECRs to energies > 55 EeV dermer . As a result, it is important to continue to explore the connection (if any) between UHECRs and Centaurus A.

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as the remaining Auger UHECR anisotropy excess and notes that continuous neutrino flux detection from such a source would require detectors larger than IceCube.

quotes.quote: There is still a remaining excess of events around the Cen A galaxy on scales of 20 degrees, see Fig.  CenA . This anisotropy has to be tested by future data.

Unfortunately those correlations were not confirmed in the new data, and the only anisotropy excess remaining in the Auger data at the highest energies is an excess around the Cen A galaxy, see Fig.  CenA .
In order to detect continuous flux from sources like Cen A one needs detectors much larger than 1 km ^3 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as the nearest active AGN and argues that a persistent UHECR clustering around it favors a light-nuclei cosmic-ray interpretation.

quotes.quote: The Virgo Cluster absence, the persistence of UHECR clustering along Cen A, the first triplet along Vela seem to confirm light nuclei UHECR understanding, implying a very narrow Universe view, even partially of galactic origin. UHECR fragments might follow (at half, fourth of the energy) the same UHECR map with a tail or a crown clustering around main UHECR group. Also secondary gamma and UHE neutrino might trace partially those maps.

Cen A (the most active and nearby AGN) is apparently shining UHECR source whose clustering (almost a quarter of the event) along a narrow solid angle around (whose opening angular size is 17^ o ) seem firm and it is favoring lightest nuclei.

sources.summary: The paper highlights a concentration of ultra-high-energy cosmic-ray events near Centaurus A, noting its proximity and the uncertainty over whether Cen A or nearby structures are responsible.

quotes.quote: A significant concentration of events is found around the location of Centaurus A (corresponding to the largest star in the left panel of fig.  swift ), which is particularly interesting because this AGN lies at only 4  Mpc from us. Figure  cena shows the number of observed events as a function of the angular distance from Cen A together with the isotropic expectations (average and 68, 95 and 99.7 expected isotropic dispersion). The most significant departure from isotropy is seen for 18^ , for which 13 events are observed while only 3 are expected. Whether these events come from Cen A or from other sources, such as from the Centaurus cluster lying behind (at 45  Mpc) is still unclear, but this is certainly a region that looks specially promising for future anisotropy searches.

sources.summary: The paper models Cen A's kiloparsec jet TeV emission and finds existing observations constrain the jet magnetic field to near or above equipartition.

quotes.quote: In this paper we explore the possibility that the TeV rays detected from the radio galaxies Cen A and M87, which both have bright, well-studied X-ray jets, are produced at least in part by inverse-Compton scattering of various photon fields by the high-energy electrons responsible for the synchrotron X-rays on kiloparsec scales. We describe a new numerical code that we have developed to carry out inverse-Compton calculations taking account of all the relevant physics and using detailed models of the jets and the photon fields in which they are embedded, and show that existing constraints on the very high-energy (VHE) ray fluxes of these two objects already place significant constraints on the magnetic field strengths in the jet in Cen A.

We have shown that the predicted inverse-Compton flux density in VHE rays on plausible models of Cen A is quite comparable to what is observed. This means that the existing TeV observations of Cen A put quite strong constraints on the magnetic field strength in the kpc-scale jet, particularly given that we have not modelled either the TeV electrons present in the compact knots in Cen A or the photon field from the presumed hidden blazar in its nucleus: B must be comparable to or larger than the equipartition field, unless our beliefs about the probable jet speed or angle to the line of sight are very wrong.

sources.summary: Cen A is used as an observed FR-I radio-galaxy benchmark, with Chandra X-ray emission, Fermi GeV lobes, and HESS core emission compared to model expectations.

quotes.quote: Extended X-ray emission from the jets and/or lobes of the radio galaxies 3C 15 , Cen A , Fornax A and M87 have been observed with Chandra . Furthermore, the Fermi Collaboration recently reported on the extended GeV emission from Cen A . Efficient particle acceleration is therefore taking place at least in some FR-Is.

Nevertheless, Fermi has recently detected some FR-I galaxies at a few hundred Mpc distances, including the extended radio lobes of Cen A (at a distance 4 Mpc), presenting fluxes similar to those predicted here. At VHE, the fluxes would be detectable by the current instruments, although the extension of the source, of tens of arcminutes at 100 Mpc, and the steepness of the spectrum above 100 GeV, may render its detection difficult. In the case of Cen A, detected by HESS , the emission seems to come only from the core, but this is expected given the large angular size of the lobes of this source, which would dilute its surface brightness, requiring very long observation times to render the source detectable.

sources.summary: The paper measures the black hole mass in Centaurus A using spectroastrometry of its rotating gas disk, finding results consistent with classical rotation-curve methods.

quotes.quote: 2, 85748 Garching bei M unchen, Germany nneumaye@eso.org Received ; accepted We measure the black hole mass in the nearby active galaxy Centaurus A (NGC 5128) using a new method based on spectroastrometry of a rotating gas disk.

In this paper we apply the spectroastrometric method to multiple longslit and integral field near infrared spectroscopic observations of Centaurus A. We find that the application of the spectroastrometric method provides results perfectly consistent with the more complex classical method based on rotation curves: the measured BH mass is nearly independent of the observational setup and spatial resolution and the spectroastrometric method allows the gas dynamics to be probed down to spatial scales of 0.02 , i.e. 1/10 of the spatial resolution and 1/50 of BH sphere of influence radius. The best estimate for the BH mass based on kinematics of the ionised gas is then (M_ BH i^2/M_ ) 7.5 0.1 which corresponds to M_ BH = 9.6^ +2.5 _ 1.8 10^ 7 M_ for an assumed disk inclination of i=35^ .

sources.summary: The review notes an Auger event excess around Centaurus A, calling it the closest AGN and a possible first identified ultrahigh-energy cosmic-ray source needing more data.

quotes.quote: An interesting possibility is the cluster of Auger events around the direction of Centaurus A, the closest AGN (at 3.8 Mpc). The most significant excess is in a 24 degree window around Cen A, where 19 of the 98 events are found, while 7.6 are expected by chance correlation . The significance for the excess region can only be established with independent data. Only much higher statistics will tell if Cen A is the first UHECR source to be identified.

sources.summary: The paper highlights Centaurus A as an active galaxy targeted for neutrino limits, motivated by cosmic-ray enhancement, while noting current Auger limits remain above model expectations.

quotes.quote: In particular, an upper limit has been set on neutrinos from the active galaxy Centaurus A. This Active Galactic Nucleus (AGN) is an interesting source, since there seems to be an enhancement of charged cosmic rays from the direction of this source . The obtained 90 .l. \ upper neutrino flux limit is shown in auger-cena . The present upper limits are still about 2 orders of magnitude above the expectations from theoretical models for this source.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Cen A as a nearby FR I radio galaxy useful for studying jets, with dust, gas, star formation, obscuration, and a narrow Fe line.

quotes.quote: Cen A : Nearby bright FR I radio galaxy with large scale relativistic jets. Galactic disk nearly edge-on, dominated by dust, gas, and star formation. Good source for studying jets; central engine heavily obscured below 5 keV markowitz:07 . There is a strong narrow Fe line and the continuum is modestly variable.

sources.summary: The paper uses Centaurus A as a nearby AGN worked example, setting its distance, enclosed mass, and Schwarzschild-radius scale for comparing proton interactions with dark matter and gas.

quotes.quote: We provide here both simple estimates and a detailed worked-out example for the case of the nearby AGN Centaurus A (CenA) to substantiate our claim.

d_ AGN is the distance to the AGN ( d_ AGN = 3.7 Mpc for the case of CenA), _ B(DM) = \ _ B(DM) () \ dr , with _ B(DM) () the baryonic (DM) density profile (the factor 1/m_ B, \ DM turns this into the actual number density).

We normalize the overall factors _0 \ r_c^ , to be of the same order of magnitude as the mass enclosed within 5 kpc from the AGN: in the case of CenA the total gravitating mass enclosed in 5 kpc is equal to 7.5 10^ 10 \ , as obtained from X-ray measurements under the hypothesis of hydrostatic equilibrium . Notice that this is, for our purposes, a conservative assumption, as the DM density is generally thought to be subdominant (if not negligible) compared to the baryonic mass in the vicinity of the AGN center (see e.g. ). To compute the factor _B in Eq. ( master ), we set r_ min =10 \ R_S , with R_S = 5 10^ 6 pc the Schwarzschild radius for CenA, and r_ max = 15 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5128/Cen A as an S0+S peculiar class-4 object whose IRS spectrum has unusually strong silicate absorption, implying heavy nuclear extinction in a dusty compact torus.

quotes.quote: NGC 5128 Cen A (S0+S pec) The most striking feature in its IRS spectrum is the strong silicate absorption feature at 9.7 m. This feature is weak or seen in emission in the other class-4 galaxies of the sample. Sargsyan11 found this feature typical of the so called ``absorption AGN'' while ``emission AGN'' have spectra similar to our MIR class-4 spectra. Such a strong absorption is an indication of an enormous amount of extinction towards the nucleus, which is probably embedded in a very dusty and compact torus see e.g. Armus07 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Cen A as a bright AGN detected across radio to TeV energies, with gamma rays from the core jet and lobes and hard-X-ray spectral fits.

quotes.quote: Cen A is one of the brightest AGN detected from radio energies up to the TeV range Aharonian2009 and in ultra-high energy cosmic rays by Auger Abraham2009 . Gamma-rays are produced by the jet in the central core and by inverse Compton scattering of microwave and infrared-optical photons in the giant radio lobes. At hard X-ray energies, Ginga and balloon-flight observations revealed a power law fit with a slope of 1.8 with a possible break at 180 keV Miyazaki1996 . OSSE, COMPTEL, and EGRET observations during an intermediate emission state showed a three-segment broken power law with breaks at E_ 1 = 150 keV and E_ 2 = 16.7 MeV and slopes of _ 1 =1.74, _ 2 =2.3 , and _ 3 =3.3 , while a fit during a low emission state showed breaks at E_ 1 = 140 keV and E_ 2 = 590 keV and slopes of _ 1 =1.73, _ 2 =2.0 and, _ 3 =2.6 Steinle1998 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Centaurus A as a Seyfert-galaxy example for MAXI power-spectrum analysis, showing observed and simulated PSDs, the interpolation contribution near the Poisson level, and the subtracted PSD.

quotes.quote: Fig fig:psd+pl is the PSD and simulated Poisson level of the Seyfert galaxy Centaurus A. In Fig fig:psd+pl (a) the black points show the observed data, and the red line shows the simulated PSD. The effect of linear interpolation appears as a small rise above the Poisson level in low frequency. The resultant PSD subtracted the simulated PSD is shown in Fig fig:psd+pl (b).

sources.summary: NGC 5128 is treated as a strong outlier with too small a black hole for its globular-cluster count; the authors adopt averaged dynamical black-hole estimates.

quotes.quote: Most notably, the Milky Way and NGC 5128 are both strong outliers in the sense that they have too-small SMBH's for their number of GCs, though both quantities are well determined.

The discordance between this and the data plotted in Figure 3 suggests that observational factors such as spatial resolution, choice of , and limiting magnitude are important but difficult to correct for in a given data set. Another way to compare the values in the literature is shown in Figure 4 where we have simply plotted the value adopted in the black hole mass determination (x-axis) vs. the HyperLeda and McElroy average values (y-axis). Interestingly, except for a few galaxies, the values are consistent within about twice the nominal 5 scatter usually adopted for .

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as a nearby AGN comparison object, using its black-hole mass, X-ray spectral fits, and hot-flow model placement.

quotes.quote: To reduce the above limitations, we gathered from literature the values of the intrinsic X-ray slopes from detailed models of broad-band data sets (including Integral , I, and/or Suzaku , S, data) of several well studied, nearby objects: NGC 4258 (S, Yamada et al. \ 2009), Circinus (S, Yang et al. \ 2009), Centaurus A (I, Beckmann et al 2011), NGC 4151 (I,S, XMM , dim and bright states; Lubi nski et al 2010) and NGC 5548 (S, Brenneman et al 2012). Crucially for precision of _ 2-10 , the first three have M estimated directly from gas or maser kinematics; for Circinus we use M=(1.7 0.3) 10^6 M_ (Greenhill et al. \ 2003), for Cen A, M = 9.6^ +2.5 _ 1.8 10^7 M_ (Gnerucci et al. \ 2011), and for NGC 4258, M = 3.6 10^7 (Miyoshi et al. \ 1995; uncertainty not reported).
The two model points located close to Cen A are for the models (a8 and o3) with very tenuous flows, with ^ PS 0.01 and kT_ e ^ PS 1 MeV, and their X-ray spectra strongly deviate from a power-law.

Fig. \ fig:comppsparam shows parameters of the COMPPS fits for NGC 4151 (Lubi nski et al. \ 2010; slab fits), Cen A (Beckmann et al. \ 2011; slab) and NGC 5548 (Brenneman et al. \ 2012; sphere fit so we plot a reduced ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Cen A as a gamma-ray LLAGN core candidate whose GeV-to-TeV spectrum and jet emission complicate isolating a RIAF component.

quotes.quote: The cores of Cen A and M87 are candidates, and both GeV and TeV gamma rays are detected fermi09sl,hess06m87,hess09cenA,sah+13 . For Cen A, the GeV gamma-ray spectrum cannot be smoothly connected to the TeV spectrum, and a break around 3 GeV has been suggested. This could indicate the existence of two components. Whereas some contributions could come from RIAFs, emission from jets are prominent tak11 , and it would not be easy to identify the RIAF component in observed spectra.

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen-A as the nearest AGN and Southern Hot Spot source, explaining its UHECR clustering through light helium nuclei bent by Galactic magnetic fields.

quotes.quote: Such a composition would indeed explain the spread angle of UHECR by their random bending along galactic fields ruling out a unique Hot Spot due to our nearest AGN: Cen-A. Moreover, He-like nuclei are fragile through photo-nuclear dissociation by the Cosmic Microwave Background: they cannot cross large GZK distances as can protons ( 50-80 Mpc), but they can fill a few smaller Universe size ( 3-4 Mpc). Because of this Cen-A could shine, Virgo could not.

On the contrary, AUGER clustering around the nearest active AGN, Cen-A (3.5 Mpc), may be caused by random incoherent bending of light He nuclei along the galactic magnetic fields; the absence of Virgo in the TA data is explained again by the UHECR He opacity.

sources.summary: The paper reports Cen A among objects with significant long-timescale Fe-K variability and >2 sigma Ni-K detection, but says the brightest XMM-Newton data suffered pile-up and were excluded.

quotes.quote: Significant Fe-K line variability of several tens percents was detected for a pair of observations separated by 1000--2000 days (Cen A, IC 4329 A, NGC 3516, and NGC 4151) and 158 days (NGC 3516). These timescales are larger by a factor of 10--100 than the inner radius of torus, consistent with the view that X-ray reflection by torus is a main origin of a narrow Fe-K line. Ni-K line was detected with >2 level for the Circinus galaxy, Cen A, MRK 3, NGC 4388, and NGC 4151.

Then, the data of the brightest object Cen A suffered significantly from the pile-up, and other objects often have a scatter of constant factors with 10 difference between MOS-1 and MOS-2, and often with a larger difference between MOS and PN. Therefore, we did not treat Cen A data here.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5128 as the peculiar radio-galaxy host of SN 1986G, with a strong merger-linked dust lane, old stellar population indicators, and ongoing star formation.

quotes.quote: SN 1986G was located in NGC 5128, also known as Centauras A, which is at a distance 3.42 0.18 Mpc Ferrarese07 .

The host system is the peculiar radio galaxy NGC 5128 which is generally classified in terms of its optical morphology as a lenticular or elliptical galaxy. This implies a dominant old stellar population, but this may well be misleading, as SN 1986G occurred in the middle of the strong dust lane which gives NGC 5128 its peculiar appearance, but well offset from the active galactic nucleus. The dust lane is generally considered to be the result of a merger with a smaller gas-rich galaxy Baade54, Tubbs80 , and it is associated with substantial ongoing star formation, which is indicated by strong, extended clumpy H emission throughout most of the dust lane see Bland87 .

sources.summary: The review describes Centaurus A as an elliptical with molecular gas shells, likely from interaction with a massive spiral, possibly affected by its radio jet.

quotes.quote: Molecular gas has also been detected in shells at a galactocentric radius of 15 kpc ( 1.16 r_ 25 ) in the elliptical galaxy Centaurus A ( charmandaris00 ). charmandaris00 calculated the mass of molecular hydrogen in the CenA shells to be M_ H_ 2 = 4.3 10^ 7 M_ . Like polar rings, shells are likely caused by galaxy interactions galaxy interactions and charmandaris00 concluded that CenA interacted with a massive spiral galaxy rather than a low-mass dwarf galaxy because of the large total gas mass and large ratio of molecular to atomic gas in CenA. Additional molecular cloud formation may have been triggered by the interaction between the shells and the CenA radio jet (see also salome16 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Cen A as a giant elliptical benchmark, noting bimodal GC metallicity peaks, radial segregation of metal-rich versus metal-poor clusters, and a broader inner stellar MDF.

quotes.quote: For instance the MDF of GCs in the MW has peaks at = 1.55 0.07 and 0.55 0.10 harris16 and the giant elliptical Cen A has peaks at =-1.4 and -0.4 beasley08 .
In Cen A the majority of the clusters with > 1 are within 20 kpc projected distance from the center, whereas the metal-poor clusters show a flatter distribution with projected distance.

We see that the stellar MDF in the inner region of Cen A is wider and extends to slightly higher than in the outer halo. harrisharris02 have argued that this is due to contributions by bulge stars. On the other hand, the MDFs of the GCs are flatter with the contribution of the metal-poor clusters with [ M/H ] < -1.5 increasing towards larger distances.

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 5128 kinematics, describing its rotation, velocity dispersion, halo behavior, and relevant photometric or interaction features in the early-type-galaxy survey.

quotes.quote: Also known as Centaurus A, it is a giant elliptical (often classified as a peculiar lenticular) in the Centaurus group. It shows clear signs of its accretion history in tidal streams and shells 1983ApJ...272L...5M,2002AJ....124.3144P,2016ApJ...823...19C , evidences of major nuclear activity see e.g. 2010PASA...27..449N , and jet-induced star forming regions e.g. 2002ApJ...564..688R . Its kinematics probed by PNe reflects the presence of regions which are not in equilibrium, showing a highly asymmetric and rich in subcomponents velocity field. The fit of the a_0 (R) parameter of the rotation model eq:fourier_definitivo in radial bins shows the presence of peculiar bulk motions in groups of PNe.

sources.summary: The paper studies Centaurus A as a nearby radio-active elliptical whose satellites form a narrow, kinematically coherent plane; 14 of 16 satellites with velocities follow a corotation pattern rare in simulations.

quotes.quote: We test this assumption using the kinematics of satellite galaxies around the Centaurus A galaxy. Our statistical analysis reveals evidence for corotation in a narrow plane: of the 16 Centaurus A's satellites with kinematic data, 14 follow a coherent velocity pattern aligned with the long axis of their spatial distribution. In standard cosmology simulations, < 0.5 of Centaurus A-like systems show such behavior.

It comprises two concentrations: the Cen A subgroup dominated by a radio-active elliptical galaxy Centaurus A (Cen A, NGC 5128) at a distance of 3.8 Mpc, and the M 83 subgroup dominated by a late-type spiral galaxy M 83 (NGC 5236) at a distance of 4.9 Mpc .

sources.summary: The paper cites Centaurus A as a clear jet-induced star-formation case, with a young stellar filament along the radio jet and molecular gas where the jet encountered an H I shell.

quotes.quote: AGN feedback due to radio jets can also be positive, and trigger star formation in the hot atmosphere of a giant elliptical galaxy. In Centaurus A, a long filament of young star formation aligned along the radio jet is known since a long time. ALMA and APEX have detected large quantities of molecular gas aligned on this H filament, just when the radio jet encountered an HI shell, cf Figure fig4 .. On the jet path, all the atomic gas has been transformed in molecules (Salom e et al. 2016).

sources.summary: The review identifies Centaurus A as an FR I radio galaxy whose extended lobes dominate the gamma-ray flux and likely emit through inverse-Compton scattering.

quotes.quote: Extended radio lobes represent another possible site of production of high-energy photons ( , ) as confirmed by the LAT detection of the lobes in the FR I radio galaxy Centaurus A ( ). The detection and imaging of extended ray emission is challenging given (i) the PSF of the LAT (the 68 containment PSF is 0.8 deg at 1 GeV) and (ii) because the emission is isotropic and not boosted by relativistic effects. Indeed, in Centaurus A it was possible to separate the contribution of the two lobes from the core in 10 months of data thanks to the proximity of the source (z=0.001825) and to the total angular extent of its lobes ( 10 deg). The flux above 100 MeV of the two lobes accounts for more than half of the total ray flux of the source and the emission is likely produced through Inverse Compton (IC) scattering of the Cosmic Microwave Background Radiation (CMB).

sources.summary: The HARMONI simulations model Centaurus A as a nearby giant elliptical, adopting old metal-rich stellar populations, surface-brightness profiles, and exposure predictions for resolved-star spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: We have produced and analysed a set of simulated data-cubes using the HSIM software, mimicking observations across the giant elliptical galaxy Centaurus A and in the nuclear bulge of the Milky Way.

Making such studies possible within a volume of 4Mpc is a fundamental step to understand the formation history of particular systems like a large elliptical galaxy (Centaurus A, hereafter CenA) and to contrast the evolution of Local Group disc galaxies to those in other environments (e.g. the Sculptor group).

CenA stellar populations For the “large early-type” representative of CenA, the star formation activity was assumed to be short and concentrated at old times (the star formation rate Is assumed to be constant from 13 to 10 Gyr ago), with metallicities distributed in the range 0.3 < [ Fe/H ] < +0.2 (0.01 < Z < 0.03) ; the halo of CenA is kwown to have a MDF peaking at M/H 0.6 (see ), but since we will be probing regions closer to the center we move towards larger metallicities. We create mock catalogues with the stellar population mix (number of stars of given magnitude and colour) expected for CenA at radius R/Reff = 1 and 2, where Reff is the effective radius, over a field of view of 6.4" 4.5" .

sources.summary: The paper treats Centaurus A as a nearby giant-lobed radio galaxy whose past powerful jet activity may have accelerated UHECRs now escaping from dormant lobes.

quotes.quote: In , we showed that the excesses above isotropy in the PAO data pierre_auger_collaboration_indication_2018 may be explained by considering strong UHECR contributions from Centaurus A and Fornax A. Both these sources show giant lobes whose total energy contents are large compared to the energy input from the currently active jet in the system. There is also evidence of declining AGN activity in Fornax A iyomoto_declined_1998,lanz_constraining_2010 and of recent merger activity in both sources mackie_evolution_1998,horellou_atomic_2001 . Together, these considerations led us to invoke a scenario in which Fornax A and Centaurus A had both had more powerful, possibly FRII-like, jet ``outburst'' in the past, during which UHECRs were accelerated, meaning that their giant lobes now act as (slowly leaking) UHECR reservoirs.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 5128 as Centaurus A in the ETG sample and notes its RAR deviation plus strong interaction signs: a dust band, stellar shells, and a twisted planetary-nebula velocity field.

quotes.quote: The data for the remaining four galaxies are available in the following papers: schuberth10 for NGC 1399, cote03 for NGC 4472 ( = M49), lee08 for NGC 4649 ( = M60), and for NGC 5128 ( = Centaurus A). The basic observational data for the galaxies in the sample is available in tabgals and the information related to GCs is available in tabgcss .

Many signs of a strong interaction in NGC 5128 (=Cen A), whose RAR deviates noticeably from the RAR of the LTGs (panel (f) of g15 ), are well known. These include the dust band quillen93 , stellar shells malin83 , or the twist in the velocity field of the planetary nebulae peng04 .

sources.summary: Centaurus A is described as the nearest AGN and a TeV radio galaxy whose core SED hardening motivates possible extra VHE components for FR 0 models.

quotes.quote: A few of them are also known as VHE emitters including M87 (d 16 Mpc), the first extra-galactic source detected at VHE energies, and Centaurus A (CenA), the nearest (d 4 Mpc) AGN to us In addition to M 87 and CenA, there are other four radio galaxies detected at TeV energies, i.e., NGC 1275 (d 76.7 Mpc), IC 310 (d 82.8 Mpc), PKS 0625-35 (d 245 Mpc) and 3C 264 (d 95.1 Mpc). .

Thus far, indications that such components could actually exist have been only found in the SED of the core of the radio galaxy Centaurus A . Interestingly, its emission deviates from the simple extrapolation of the < 3 GeV spectrum observed by Fermi , displaying a clear hardening at < 250 GeV ( and references therein). A comprehensive discussion concerning the processes that could explain the origin of a TeV component is presented in .

sources.summary: The paper adopts NGC 5128’s stellar-kinematic black hole mass, notes other gas and stellar kinematic estimates, and finds its estimate consistent with those results.

quotes.quote: Finally, the M_ BH of NGC 5128 is adopted from Cap2009 , in which the authors used stellar kinematics to obtain M_ BH value. Therefore, we have thirteen M_ BH estimates in total for ten type 1 and three type 2 AGNs , which will be further used in our M_ BH and _ investigations.

Notes: NGC 5128 has also black hole mass estimates from the other methods: M_ BH 4.5 ^ +1.7 _ 1.0 10 ^ 7 M _ from H _ 2 gas kinematics by Neumayer , M_ BH = 5.5 3.0 10 ^ 7 M _ from stellar kinematics by Cap2009 . We emphasize that our M_ BH estimate for NGC 5128 is consistent with these results.

sources.summary: The paper presents Centaurus A as a local AGN feedback laboratory, with jet-aligned outflow cones and star-forming blobs in the ionisation cone that may trace positive feedback.

quotes.quote: In Centaurus A (1" 18.5 pc) the outflow is mainly distributed in two cones (direction north-east and south-west) in the same direction of the extended double-sided jet revealed both in the radio and X-rays (e.g. hardcastle2003 ), and located perpendicularly with respect to the gas in the disc component.

Centaurus A: a local laboratory to study AGN positive feedback Centaurus A shows the best example of a radio jet emitted by the central AGN interacting with the ISM (e.g. santoro2016 and references therein).

The galaxy FOV South-West portion hosts a blob (solid circle) with a velocity consistent with the gas disc and by Composite and SF dominated ionisation (in and BPT, respectively), that can be interpreted as SF triggered in the ionisation cone due to compression of the galaxy ISM by the outflow, as already revealed in another galaxy of the MAGNUM survey, NGC 5643, by cresci2015b . Moreover, a nearby clump (solid square) appears to have SF ionisation, but velocities consistent with the gas in the outflow, suggesting that newborn stars could be forming directly in outflowing gas (see maiolino2017 ).

sources.summary: The paper presents Cen A as a discrepant high-cutoff AGN case, excluding BAT data and noting that a relativistic jet may contribute without affecting the population median.

quotes.quote: Cen A -- The discrepancy between the based constraint from ricci+2017-bass , which is in the _e > 3 regime ( 70 10 keV), and nearly all other studies implying a very high \ (including this paper and fuerst+2016-cenA , based on high-quality \ data), is probably the best example of the spurious occurrence of degenerate _e > 3 constraints. The cause for this may be the curvature of the \ spectrum, which does not match the very flat \ spectrum. For this reason we excluded \ data from our fitting already in Paper I as it was impossible to find an acceptable model otherwise. The extremely high \ may be indicative of a contribution from the relativistic jet in this AGN, so we test its exclusion from the sample in sec:discussion-sysunc-excl , finding negligible impact on the population median.

sources.summary: The paper studies Cen A's satellite system, adding VLT/MUSE velocities for twelve dwarfs and finding coherent motion among 21 of 28 satellites, a structure rare in simulated Cen A analogs.

quotes.quote: A similar case has been identified around the nearby elliptical galaxy Centaurus A (Cen A ). In this Letter, we study the satellite system of Cen A adding twelve new galaxies with line-of-sight velocities from VLT/MUSE observations. We find 21 out of 28 dwarf galaxies with measured velocities share a coherent motion. Similarly flattened and coherently moving structures are found only in 0.2 of Cen A analogs in the Illustris-TNG100 cosmological simulation, independently of whether we use its dark-matter-only or hydrodynamical run.

sources.summary: The paper models Centaurus A as an FR I radio galaxy whose large-scale jet has VHE emission and a multi-wavelength SED explainable with shear-accelerated electrons.

quotes.quote: FR I jet: Centaurus A Centaurus A (NGC 5128) is an FR I radio galaxy located at a distance of 3.8 Mpc Harris2010 . Radio observations of the jet's proper motion on the sub-kpc scale suggest that the jet is moving with a mildly relativistic speed of _0 0.5 Hardcastle2003,Snios2019 . Recently, the High Energy Stereoscopic System (H.E.S.S.) has detected extended very-high-energy (VHE) emission from the large-scale jet of Centaurus A HESS2020 . In Figure fig:2 , we show an exemplary multi-wavelength-SED fit of the large-scale jet emission of Centaurus A.

sources.summary: NGC 5128 (Cen A) is described as an S0 peculiar galaxy hosting FXRT 3, with a probable association and local counterpart candidates consistent with an older environment.

quotes.quote: FXRT 3/XRT 070530 (cataloged as an X-ray source by Liu2011 and Wang2016 , although never classified as an FXRT) is located in the S0 peculiar galaxy NGC 5128 (Cen A; m_ R 6.3 AB mag; 3.1 Mpc), at an angular (projected) distance of 5 5 ( 5.0 kpc). The probability of this association occurring by chance is 1.3 10 ^ 5 based on the FXRT--galaxy offset and the space density of m_ R < 12 AB mag galaxies, thus implying a highly probable association; the binomial probability that this FXRT is a background source is 1.7 10 ^ 2 , reinforcing an association with NGC 5128 (see Sect. sec:population for more details). There are several dozen possible faint counterpart candidates within the 3 X-ray error region in the HST F606W and F814W images (typically m_ F606W and m_ F814W 25 AB mag; see Fig. fig:image_cutouts ), of which one very red object stands out near the center ( source 1 in Fig. fig:image_cutouts ) with m_ F606W = 25.4 and m_ F814W = 22.1 AB mag M_ F606W = 2.1 and M_ F814W = 5.4 AB mag, respectively; taken from the HSCv3; Whitmore2016 or from DECam m_ z = 22.3 and m_ y = 21.7 AB mag ( M_ z = 5.2 and M_ y = 5.7 AB mag), which might be typical of either a small globular cluster or a red supergiant star. Based on the lack of young stars in the local host environment, we associate FXRT 3 with the former.

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as an important radio-galaxy benchmark, noting its low gamma/X-ray ratio, outlier high-energy SED behavior, and extended lobe/kpc-scale gamma-ray emission.

quotes.quote: Radio galaxies may contribute to the cosmic MeV gamma-ray background comparable to blazars if gamma-ray-quiet radio galaxies have X-ray to gamma-ray spectra like Cen A, with a small gamma-ray to X-ray flux ratio.
Note that the outlier object with a low peak frequency of 10^ 19.7 Hz and a low luminosity of 10^ 41.5 erg s ^ 1 is Cen A.

Cen A and Fornax A show extended gamma-ray emission from their radio lobes Abdo10d, Acke16 . Cen A also shows the existence of kpc-scale extended gamma-ray emission Prok19,HESS20 , where various models are proposed to explain the origin .

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as the strongest southern target for globular-cluster FRB searches, balancing fewer clusters than M87 against greater proximity and MeerKAT detectability.

quotes.quote: Table table:FRBrates establishes M87 and Cen A as the most promising galaxies for detecting globular cluster FRBs, in the north and south, respectively. Given that M87 has by far the most globular clusters ( N_ cl 10^4 ) of any nearby galaxy (see column 6 of Table table:rates ), this is not a surprise. Although Cen A contains a factor of roughly 10 fewer clusters relative to M87, its relative proximity enables lower luminosity bursts to be detected.
Similarly, we predict that a 50 hr ( 1.7 hr) MeerKat survey of Cen A has a 90 chance of detecting a single burst for =-1.5 ( =-2.4 ).

sources.summary: The paper compares RMHD shear-acceleration simulations with Centaurus A, finding a model case and spine velocity compatible with its observed large-scale jet emission.

quotes.quote: A synchrotron X-ray origin is certain in the case of the low-power Fanaroff–Riley (FR) I radio galaxy Centaurus A, as the same electrons produce the diffuse TeV emission HESS2020 .

In our previous studies, estimates of the magnetic field and the coefficient w have been obtained for 3C 273 and Centaurus A by fitting their multi-wavelength SEDs see Table1 of Wang2021MNRAS . Our results here suggest that Centaurus A and 3C 273 can be approximately described by V6B-2 case and V9B-3 case, respectively. Taking W_ sh /R_ jet =1/2 (3/4) for Centaurus A (3C 273), w =15 (4.7) requires _0=0.5 (0.87) . These velocities are consistent with radio observations, that indicate _0 0.5 for Centaurus A Hardcastle2003,Snios2019 and _0<0.94 for 3C 273 Meyer2016 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as a local FR-I radio galaxy and UHECR candidate, noting its limited current jet power and models requiring earlier stronger activity.

quotes.quote: They have been commonly suggested as UHECR sources , and the local radio galaxy Centaurus A (Cen A) is a particularly compelling candidate .

This has lead various authors to consider local FR-I radio galaxies such as Cen A and Fornax A, which also have compelling associations with UHECR anisotropies ; however, in Cen A the current jet power is thought to be 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 and the bow-shock associated with the current activity is not fast or powerful to accelerate UHECRs .

The motivation here is that Cen A is unique in the CoG structure in having powerful AGN jet activity, and the model provides a way for arrival directions to be correlated with star-forming galaxies without requiring acceleration in the star-forming galaxies themselves. A workable model requires Cen A to have had a UHECR luminosity 20 Myr ago that is 200 times that currently reaching Earth from the source, and also necessitates magnetic field strengths of 10-20 nG out to a distance of 400-800 kpc in the CGM.

sources.summary: The review highlights Cen A's kiloparsec jet as an extended VHE emitter whose emission can be modeled with weak magnetization, dust inverse-Compton scattering, and shear acceleration.

quotes.quote: The recent detection of extended VHE emission along the kpc-scale jet of Cen A has provided an important validation to this picture, see Fig. HESS_CenA , by directly tracing the electrons and removing the degeneracy ( B, ) inherent in an X-ray synchrotron model. IC up-scattering of dust by ultra-relativistic (synchrotron X-ray emitting) electrons with _e =10^8 accounts for the observed VHE emission and confirms the X-ray synchrotron interpretation. The results imply a jet that is only weakly magnetized ( <0.5 ) on kiloparsec scales.

A recent application shows that in the presence of a Kolmogorov-type ^ 1/3 , the SED of the kpc-scale jet in Cen A can be successfully reproduced within a linear shear model for spine velocities of v_j 0.7 and shear layer widths of 100 pc .

sources.summary: The review treats Centaurus A as a nearby FRI-type jet source whose X-rays are synchrotron, excludes it from the variability sample because of prior evidence, and notes its deep Chandra coverage.

quotes.quote: Assuming a leptonic jet model, the X-ray emission of most low-power or FRI class sources, including nearby sources such as M87 harris2003_sed and Cen A hardcastle2007 , are often (but not always) well-described by synchrotron radiation from a single electron population which extends from radio to optical and X-ray energies.

Two sources (3C 305 and 3C 171) were excluded as the extended X-ray emission associated has been attributed to jet-driven gas hardcastle2012_3c305,hardcastle2010_3c171 ; we also exclude the sources M87 and Centaurus A, which are already known to produce X-rays by synchrotron process feigelson1981,kraft2002,wilson2000 . Both M87 and Centaurus A have been far more deeply and frequently observed than typical of the remaining sample (with 50 and 43 distinct observations, respectively), and this is likely one of the reasons that X-ray variability has been reported in both cases.
Unlike the jets of M87 and Centaurus A, however, the X-ray emission in Pictor A is clearly from a second emission component hardcastle2005 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Centaurus A as hosting a thick, possibly co-rotating satellite plane and having disturbed morphology attributed to a high-mass-ratio merger about 2 Gyr ago.

quotes.quote: Beyond the Local Group, Centaurus A has been found to also host a plane-of-satellites Tully2015cena, Muller2016cena , which is oriented nearly edge-on to the Milky Way and may be rotationally supported Muller2021cena . The plane is notable not solely due to its thickness, which at around 130 kpc Muller2019casp is significantly wider than the VPOS and GPoA, but in conjunction with a strong implied degree of co-rotation from the system's radial velocities Muller2018casp, Muller2021casp .

Centaurus A's disturbed morphology is argued to be a result of a 1/1.5 merger 2 Gyr ago Wang2020merger - the CASP is thick (around 130 kpc ; Muller2019casp ) and richly populated, which may be a consequence of a high-mass ratio merger event.

It is still conceivable that the observed Centaurus A plane -- flattened not by a thin absolute plane height but from an extended radial distribution Muller2019casp - may have been formed from its proposed merger 2 Gyr ago Wang2020merger .

sources.summary: The paper describes Centaurus A as one of the nearby galaxies whose satellites form rotating planar structures, contributing to a high-significance challenge for CDM models.

quotes.quote: (T4a) The Impossible Local Group: Disks of Satellites / Planes of Satellites : The Galaxy LyndenBell1976, Kroupa+2005, Metz+2008, PawlowskiKroupa2020 , Andromeda Koch+2006, Metz+2007, Ibata+2013 , Centaurus A Tully+2015, Mueller+2021 , M81 Chiboukas+2013 , NGC 253 Martinez+2021 and the galaxy pair NGC 4490/85 (Karachentsev Kroupa, submitted) and most other galaxies (Ibata et al. 2014 ) have a large fraction of their satellite galaxies arranged in vast rotating planar structures. Since the orbits of the satellites are confined to disks, these systems are reminiscent of planetary systems requiring a dissipational process for their formation rather than the spheroidal distribution of dark-matter bearing satellite galaxies expected from the merger tree in the SMoC (Pawlowski 2021a,b ).
The combined tension of only three (the Galaxy, Andromeda and Centaurus A) existing simultaneously is above the 5 sigma confidence (Asencio et al. 2022, ).

Ignoring that the Milky Way, Andromeda and Cen A actually have more satellite galaxies in their disk configurations than only 11 in each, observing three such independent plane-of-satellite systems simultaneously in our immediate cosmological vicinity would have a probability of 8 10^ 8 (consistent with the more accurate calculation available in Asencio et al. 2022 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Centaurus A as its AGN MIR template, resolving hot central continuum with absent UIBs from star-forming disk emission and linking this to AGN-heated dust.

quotes.quote: Several studies have already shown that MIR spectra in AGNs also present weak UIBs ( , , , , ). This effect is demonstrated in the spectrum of the nearest AGN of our sample located in the radio galaxy Centaurus A (CenA, NGC 5128, ). In the MIR observations of CenA, we have sufficient spatial resolution to disentangle the emission of the central regions near the AGN from that due to star forming regions of the galactic disk. The absence of UIBs in the central 5 arcsec ( 100 pc) possibly indicates the destruction of their carriers by the intense UV-X-ray radiation field from the AGN ( , ). Moreover, there is a noticeable continuum at short wavelengths (3-10 m) commonly attributed to hot dust, associated with the torus of molecular gas proposed in the unified model ( , , ).

sources.summary: The paper models Centaurus A as a possible UHECR source, finding 0.3 microgauss fields give too little deflection while 1 microgauss fields only marginally match the spectrum.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The source Cen--A is located at RA =201.3^o , =-43.0^o in equatorial coordinates, and corresponds to the peak of flux in Figs. F1 , F2 .

quotes.quote: We present numerical simulations for energy spectra and angular distributions of nucleons above 10^ 19 eV injected by the radio-galaxy Centaurus A at a distance 3.4 Mpc and propagating in extra-galactic magnetic fields in the sub-micro Gauss range. We show that field strengths B 0.3 G , as proposed by Farrar Piran, cannot provide sufficient angular deflection to explain the observational data. A magnetic field of intensity B 1 G could reproduce the observed large-scale isotropy and could marginally explain the observed energy spectrum. However, it would not readily account for the E=320 93 EeV Fly's Eye event that was detected at an angle 136 ^o away from Cen--A.

sources.summary: The paper describes Centaurus A as the nearest AGN and a luminous radio galaxy with an optically obscured nucleus, silicate absorption, strong lines, AGN ionization, and PAH features.

quotes.quote: Centaurus A is the closest AGN and a luminous radio galaxy but with a nucleus completely obscured optically; a mid-infrared ISO spectrum of the nucleus is in mir99 . This source is resolved along the slit of the IRS, but the spectrum shown is only that of the central nucleus within the same IRS spatial resolution as an unresolved source. Strong silicate absorption is present in Figure 5, and the emission lines are also strong. The strength of [ Ne V ] indicates ionization by the AGN, but PAH features are also present.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5128 (Centaurus A) as the nearest giant elliptical, emphasizing its roughly 1000 globular clusters, low specific frequency, and unusually large nearby globular cluster system.

quotes.quote: At a distance of 4 Mpc (Soria et al. , Rejkuba , Harris et al. ), NGC 5128 (Centaurus A) is the closest giant elliptical (gE) galaxy (see Israel for a review). It possesses a rather low specific frequency of globular clusters (GCs), with S_N=1.4 0.2 (Harris et al. , hereafter HHG), yet still hosts around 1000 GCs. This makes its globular cluster system (GCS) the largest of any galaxy known within 15 Mpc. It is therefore a prime target for studies of extragalactic GCSs.

sources.summary: The paper finds spherical Bondi accretion underpredicts NGC5128 by over nine orders of magnitude, treats it as an AGN comparison case, and still finds its modeled luminosity too low.

quotes.quote: In an extreme case of NGC5128(Cen A) where emitted X-ray luminosity differ of more than 9 orders of magnitude, our model is not appropriate. In this source calculated efficiency of accretion is =L_ X,observed / M _ Bondi ^2=0.32 . Because NGC5128(Cen A) is an active galactic nucleus, given by P2005 for comparison, the result is reliable.
Modeled luminosity of NGC5128 is too low in comparision with observations.

sources.summary: The paper reviews Centaurus A as the previous strong radio-galaxy shock case, describing its hot shell around the southwest inner lobe, high shell temperature, and FRI morphology with unexpectedly supersonic lobes.

quotes.quote: Kraft et al. (2003) detected a bright shell of hot gas surrounding the south-west inner lobe of the nearest radio galaxy Centaurus A. Fig. cena shows more recent Chandra data illustrating the sharp X-ray shell. The shell has a temperature ten times higher than that of the surrounding interstellar medium, and the total thermal energy of the shell is a significant fraction of the energy of the ISM. Centaurus A has an FRI morphology, so would traditionally have been expected to have subsonically expanding lobes; the detection of strongly shocked gas in this system has highlighted the fact that energy input via shocks is likely to be important in the early stages of expansion for all types of radio galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper classifies Centaurus A as the lone BL Lac among the Auger-correlated VCV galaxies and notes its nearby distance, jets, plumes, and variable X-ray flux.

quotes.quote: Hoeft, in progress.) Centaurus A Centaurus A (NGC 5128) Israel1998 is a well known, nearby (3.4 Mpc) BL Lac. Its nuclear activity is manifested by large radio/X-ray jets - radio plumes extending to 250 kpc, and a compact circumnuclear disk. Rothschild2006 report a 2-10 keV X-ray flux which varies between 1.69 - 3.23 10 ^ 10 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in six observations between August, 1996 and February, 2004 with the PCU2 instrument on RXTE .

sources.summary: The paper targeted Centaurus A as a nearby active galaxy that might account for Pierre Auger UHE cosmic-ray events, designing lunar Cherenkov observations to increase exposure toward it.

quotes.quote: Our May observing period targeted Centaurus A only, a nearby active galaxy which could potentially account for multiple UHE CR events as observed by the Pierre Auger observatory .
We therefore pointed the antennas at that part of the lunar limb closest to Cen A in order to maximise sensitivity to UHE particles from this region, as described in Ref. \ .
Table ATCA_exposures_table compares the exposure from these observations to Cen A and Sgr A with that from RICE , and our estimates for GLUE.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC5128 as a Seyfert galaxy correlated with two Auger cosmic-ray events and located in the well known Cen A region.

quotes.quote: The galaxies which correlate with the arrival directions of the cosmic rays, are NGC300 (spiral galaxy type SA), NGC4945 (Sy2), NGC5102 (HII) and NGC5128 (Sy2). With exception of NGC300, they mostly show nuclear activity and both Seyfert galaxies are included in the AGN catalogue used by 2008APh....29..188T . In particular NGC5128, which is associated with 2 cosmic-ray events, is inside the well known Cen A region.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 1322 428, alias Cen A, as a detected TANAMI radio galaxy and the closest AGN, one of only two radio galaxies in the sample detected by Fermi LAT.

quotes.quote: The two detected radio galaxies are PKS 0625 354 (OH 342) and PKS 1322 428 (Cen A).
Cen A is the closest AGN and PKS 0625 354 is interesting because it exhibits an FR I radio-galaxy morphology whereas its optical spectrum is similar to that of a BL Lac object Wills2004 .

All BL Lac objects and a large fraction of quasars (71 ) are detected in the ray regime, whereas from the 11 radio galaxies only the closest one (Cen A) and one (PKS 0625 354) with an optical spectrum similar to a BL Lac object were detected with Fermi LAT.

sources.summary: The paper cites Centaurus A as having a Spitzer-observed bipolar shell about 500 pc from its nucleus, a few Myr old and plausibly powered by nuclear star formation or AGN activity.

quotes.quote: The Spitzer Space Telescope has found a shell-like, bipolar structure in Centaurus A , 500 pc to the north and south of the nucleus, in the mid-infrared Quillen:2006 . The shell has been estimated to be a few million years old and its mechanical energy of 10^ 53-55 erg depends on the expanding velocity. A small, few-thousand solar mass nuclear burst of star formation, or an AGN origin has been proposed to explain the formation of the shell.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Centaurus A as a nearby gamma-ray loud AGN correlated with UHECR arrival directions, emphasizing it as the primary candidate production site among the nearby 1LAC AGNs.

quotes.quote: We identify 7 `` ray loud'' AGNs which are associated with UHECRs within 17^ and are likely candidates for the production sites of UHECRs: Centaurus A, NGC 4945, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 1218, RX J0008.0+1450 and NGC 253.

Within the distance _1 , 4 PAO events correlate with 3 AGNs: NGC 4945, Centaurus A and 4C+04.77; at _2 , 7 PAO events correlate with 4 AGNs: NGC 4945, Centaurus A, ESO 323-G77 and 4C+04.77; at _3 , 17 events are associated with 7 AGNs: NGC 4945, Centaurus A, ESO 323-G77, 4C+04.77, NGC 253, NGC 1218 and RXJ0008.0+1450.

Given the available information on these AGNs, our analysis suggests that Centaurus A is the primary candidate for the production site of several UHECRs observed in the direction of its group of galaxies.

sources.summary: The review discusses Cen A as a possible UHECR source direction, noting PAO clustering claims but emphasizing their uncertain significance and the nearby matter concentration.

quotes.quote: The evidence in the PAO data for a clustering of events in the region around Cen A has triggered much discussion (see discussion and references in ). However, it is difficult to quantify the level of significance of the evidence for clustering, since it is based on an a posteriori analysis, as noted in . Moreover, one must keep in mind that Cen A lies in front of one of the largest concentrations of matter in the local ( d 50 Mpc), Universe, l=-51^ ,b=19^ , so that an excess of events from that direction does not necessarily imply that Cen A is the source.

sources.summary: The paper uses Centaurus A as the nearby radio-galaxy benchmark whose lobe gamma-ray emission motivates high-energy electrons and IC/CMB interpretation in radio lobes.

quotes.quote: Ajello 2 Harvard, Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory, 60 Garden Street, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA SLAC National Laboratory and Kavli Institute for Particle Astrophysics and Cosmology, 2575 Sand Hill Road, Menlo Park, CA 94025, USA The recent discovery of the ray emission from the lobes of the closest radio galaxy Centaurus A by implies the presence of high-energy electrons at least up to 10^5 10 ^6 .

The first mechanism is responsible for the lobe radio emission, while the latter describes their observed high-energy emission in the X-rays, and recently, also in the rays as in the nearby case of Centaurus A (Abdo et al. 2010a).

Consequently, substituting l 10 ^ 2 R_ lobe 10 ^ 21 cm into Equation (3), and assuming B/B_ CMB 1, _ max is of the order of 10 ^5 10 ^6 , as required to interpret the ray emission of Centaurus A lobes as IC/CMB and in agreement with the estimate of O'Sullivan et al. (2009).

sources.summary: The paper argues that the radio galaxy Cen A, via a relativistic shock from its active black hole jet, can supply extragalactic cosmic rays up to the highest observed energies.

quotes.quote: We show here that just our Galaxy and the radio galaxy Cen A, each with their own galactic cosmic ray particles, but with those from the radio galaxy pushed up in energy by a relativistic shock in the jet emanating from the active black hole, are sufficient to describe the most recent data in the energy range PeV to near ZeV.

We revisit here an idea originally proposed in 1993 (Biermann 1993; Stanev et al. 1993) and we show how our Galaxy and the radio galaxy Cen A can describe the energy spectrum from 10 PeV up to 3 10^ 20 eV and describe the galactic to extragalactic transition at the same time.

In order to describe the data (Pierre Auger Coll. 2010a) the high energy model presented here requires minimal interaction along the path between the radio galaxy Cen A and us, and so indeed near isotropic scattering in the magnetic wind of our Galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5128/Centaurus A as a nearby early-type galaxy undergoing a poorly mixed merger whose dust lanes obscure central optical and soft X-ray regions.

quotes.quote: NGC 5128 Centaurus A (NGC 5128, Cen A) is the nearest optically luminous large early-type galaxy, situated at a distance of 3.7 Mpc (Ferrarese et al. 2007), with M_B = -21.1. A small late-type galaxy is currently merging with Cen A, however, the galaxies remain poorly mixed (Quillen et al. 2006). The merger has resulted in the presence of vast dust lanes that obscure many of the central regions at optical and soft X-ray wavelengths (Graham 1979).

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as a radio galaxy whose Fermi gamma-ray emission was previously fit with Compton-supported pair cascades and confirms that cascade synchrotron radiation remains negligible for those parameters.

quotes.quote: In previous papers, we investigated the Compton emission from VHE ray induced pair cascades, deflected by moderate magnetic fields, in a largely model-independent way, and demonstrated that this emission can explain the Fermi fluxes and spectra of the radio galaxies Cen A and NGC 1275. In this paper, we describe a generalization of our Monte-Carlo cascade code to include the angle-dependent synchrotron output from the cascades, allowing for the application to situations with non-negligible magnetic fields, leading to potentially observable synchrotron signatures, but still not dominating the radiative energy loss of cascade particles. We confirm that the synchrotron radiation from the cascades in NGC 1275 and Cen A are negligible for the parameters used in our previous works.
We confirm that synchrotron radiation from the cascades is negligible in NGC 1275 and Cen A for the parameters we used in our previous works.

sources.summary: The review cites Cen A as a nearby large radio galaxy whose GeV and TeV inverse-Compton detections constrain lobe and jet magnetic fields near equipartition.

quotes.quote: Fermi recently made the first detection of what appears to be inverse-Compton scattering of the CMB to GeV energies, in the nearby, and therefore very large in angular size, radio galaxy Cen A 2010Sci...328..725A : taken at face value the detection implies a magnetic field strength in the lobes that is again close to the equipartition value.

At higher energies still, existing detections with TeV imaging instruments such as HESS and MAGIC place limits on inverse-Compton scattering (predominantly of starlight: 2003ApJ...597..186S ) by the TeV electrons in the jets of low-power radio galaxies: for example, existing HESS detections of Cen A, whose synchrotron properties are very well known (e.g. \ 2007ApJ...670L..81H and refs therein) already require that the magnetic field cannot be too much lower than the equipartition value, although detailed modelling is complex 2011MNRAS.415..133H . The resolution and sensitivity that will be achieved with instruments such as the planned Cerenkov Telescope Array (CTA) will actually allow imaging of the TeV inverse-Compton jets in objects such as Cen A, if B B_ eq , and so will permit mapping of the B field in an X-ray synchrotron jet for the first time.

sources.summary: The paper treats Centaurus A as an active galaxy and possible UHECR source, comparing Auger neutrino limits with theoretical jet and core production models.

quotes.quote: Limits for the particular case of the active galaxy Centaurus A, a potential source of UHECRs, are shown in Figure fig:CenA_limits , together with constraints from other experiments. The predicted fluxes for two theoretical models of UHE production -- in the jets and close to the core of Centaurus A - are also shown for comparison. The expected number of events in our blind search samples for a flux like in is about 0.1 and 0.02 for the ES and DG selection respectively, the expected number for being one order of magnitude smaller.

sources.summary: The review presents Centaurus A as the nearest radio galaxy, with GeV lobe/core emission and TeV nuclear or inner-jet emission tied to particle acceleration.

quotes.quote: The closest radio galaxy Centaurus A is at a distance of 3.7 Mpc, often considered as an ``AGN under the microscope''. observes GeV emission from the giant radio lobes and the core while H.E.S.S. detected TeV emission from the nucleus/inner jet (Abdo et al. 2010, Aharonian et al. 2009). This triggered ideas that the giant lobes are the sites of high-energy particle acceleration and production of ultra-high-energy cosmic rays (Hardcastle et al. 2009).

sources.summary: The atlas gives Cen A's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: Cen A is the closest and one of the best-studied AGN, hosted in a S0 peculiar galaxy ( D = 3.8 0.1 Mpc; harris_distance_2010 ; see israel_centaurus_1998,morganti_many_2010 for reviews). It is usually treated as a radio-loud Sy 2 (e.g., NED), albeit being completely obscured in the optical. Cen A has a supergalactic-scale FR I radio morphology with biconical lobes and a twin jet (inner PA 55 ; e.g., schreier_detection_1981 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds Cen A is a radio-loud exception with marginal spectral hardening as flux rises, matching Suzaku harder-when-brighter behavior attributed to a brighter jet hard tail.

quotes.quote: There is no correlation between flux and spectral variations for radio loud objects, with the exception of Mrk 421 and Cen A, which exhibit a marginal spectral hardening with increasing flux. Cen A has been observed to present a harder-when-brighter spectrum also during Suzaku observations fukazawa11 . This behavior has been interpreted as due to the increased contribution in the brighter state of a hard tail produced in the jet.

sources.summary: Cen A is treated as a bright radio galaxy with a clear Fe-K line and a hard-X-ray excess smoothly connected to its GeV continuum.

quotes.quote: Variability constrains were used for example in the Suzaku study of Cen A by fuk11b , who claimed a dominant jet contribution at energies above 100 keV.
Among our sample of targets, four HERGs including Cen A, NGC 1275, 3C 120, and 3C 111 reveal clear Fe-K lines, while the objects classified as LERGs --- namely M 87, , , and NGC 6251 --- do not.
However, Cen A is characterized by an excess in hard X-rays (above 100 keV) that smoothly connects to the GeV continuum of the source fuk11b .

sources.summary: The paper uses Centaurus A as a UHECR comparison case, citing Auger excess events around the radio galaxy and previous magnetic-field propagation studies of its possible contribution.

quotes.quote: An excess of UHECR events around the nearby radio galaxy Centaurus A was observed by the Pierre Auger Observatory (PAO)  . Yuksel2012 assumed that Centaurus A was the source of the excess, they and used the angular distribution of the excess events to constrain the extragalactic magnetic fields. Takami2012 and Farrar2013 examined the possible contribution of Centaurus A to the observed hotspot by simulating the propagation of UHECR protons and nuclei in the magnetic field.

sources.summary: The paper uses Cen A as a low-luminosity AGN benchmark, comparing its intrinsic X-ray spectrum, model-point location, and COMPPS fit to hot-flow predictions.

quotes.quote: The values of the intrinsic and L_ 2-10 are taken from Lubi nski et al. \ (2010) for the dim and bright states of NGC 4151, Brenneman et al. \ (2012) for NGC 5548, Beckmann et al. \ (2011) for Centaurus A, Yamada et al. \ (2009) for NGC 4258 and Yang et al. \ (2009) for Circinus; see N14 for details.

The two model points located close to Cen A are for the models (with =0.5 ) with very tenuous flows, having 0.01 and kT_ e 1 MeV; extension of this model to lower L can approximately reproduce the relation assessed in NGC 7213 if the average slope of the X-ray spectrum is considered, however, these X-ray spectra strongly deviate from a power-law.
We use the COMPPS fits for NGC 4151 (Lubi nski et al. \ 2010), Cen A (Beckmann et al. \ 2011) and NGC 5548 (Brenneman et al. \ 2012).

sources.summary: The paper describes Cen A as a nearby peculiar elliptical at 3.66 Mpc and frames the surrounding satellite planes as distributions around, and possibly influenced by, Cen A.

quotes.quote: The Cen A Group is very nearby, with the peculiar elliptical Cen A itself at 3.66 Mpc.
A familiar pattern is seen in the distribution of galaxies around Cen A.

Both entities would have been stretched out parallel to the SGZ-plane by radial repulsion from the void, and they could either be just now coming together or had a crossing, in which case Cen A would have strongly influenced the distributions of both planes.

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 5128 as a nearby giant elliptical whose globular cluster system contains about 1000 clusters and is difficult but reliably measured.

quotes.quote: An extreme example is the uncovering of the GCS in the nearby giant elliptical NGC 5128 holding a system of 1000 GCs harris_etal2004a, harris_etal2004b, harris_etal2012 . This target in some sense represents the most difficult combination of all the selection techniques listed above: the surrounding field has large amounts of contamination from both foreground stars and faint background galaxies; spatial resolution is not as effective since the GCs are marginally resolved under good ground-based seeing; and the galaxy is so nearby that its GCS is spread across a large projected area of the sky. Even so, reliable results for the GC total N_ GC and GCLF have been obtained, with several studies showing mutual consistency gharris2010 .

sources.summary: The paper compares M 87 with Cen A, describing Cen A as a gamma-ray FR-I radio galaxy whose SED can be modeled with SSC emission but includes a strong Seyfert contribution.

quotes.quote: The FR-I radio galaxy Cen A has also been observed in the gamma-ray band, and similar to M 87 the overall SED can also be modelled by a simple SSC process Abdo2010CenAcore , although a strong Seyfert contribution is visible in the X-ray and optical domain Beckmann2013 . Abdo2010CenAcore present several SED fits to the core of Cen A. Comparing the results for M 87 (jet angle =15 ^ ) to the model for Cen A (jet angle =30 ^ ), M 87 displays a slightly higher Doppler factor of =3.9 , compared to the Doppler factor used for Cen A ( = 1 ) due to the smaller jet angle .

sources.summary: The paper considers Centaurus A as an FR I galaxy with Seyfert-like X-ray emission and gamma rays above 4 GeV that may probe hot-flow regions near jet formation.

quotes.quote: We also took into account the FR I galaxy Centaurus A , whose X-ray radiation may be dominated by a Seyfert-like emission.
The component dominating the core emission of Centaurus A above 4 GeV may come from a hot flow in which _ p 0.1 - 0.2 .

We also note that in this hot flow scenario for Centaurus A, the observed rays (above 4 GeV) provide information about the region close to the jet formation site according to the radio imaging of M 87, e.g. , and assuming that the sizes of such sites are common in AGNs .

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5128 as a prominent nearby early-type galaxy with rich ISM, an accreted gas-rich dusty disk, and ongoing star formation in H II regions.

quotes.quote: NGC 5128 is the most prominent nearby example of an ETG with a rich ISM e.g., Parkin12 . What is prominent in NGC 5128 is the gaseous dusty disk, which has been associated with the remnant of an accreted gas--rich galaxy. Moreover, star formation is ongoing in NGC 5128 in the form of H ii regions Moellenhoff81 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Cen A as a radio AGN with an X-ray-peaked SED; its water-ion profiles indicate inverse P Cygni structure and possible gas infall toward the central AGN.

quotes.quote: Finally Cen A (NGC 5128) is a radio AGN with \ = 4.7 11 \ and an SED which peaks in the X-ray band.
The nearby radio galaxy Cen A appears to show inverse P Cygni profiles in the \ and \ lines, although the S/N ratio is limited.
The inverse P Cygni profile is a sign of gas infall, which in the case of Cen A may well be toward the central AGN.

sources.summary: The paper lacks a published stellar-halo mass for Cen A but uses its 30 kpc metallicity and groups it with massive classical-bulge galaxies whose halos are merger-consistent.

quotes.quote: We add stellar halo masses and metallicities at 30 kpc along the minor axis for the MW bh16,sesar11,xue15 , M31 ibata14,gilbert14 and NGC 3115 peacock15 . peacock15 estimated a mass in low metallicity stars in NGC 3115 of 1.5 10^ 10 M_ , which they adopted as an estimate of the stellar halo mass. As there is a considerable population of high metallicity stars at large radii in NGC 3115 which could be argued to legitimately belong to its stellar halo, we choose to adopt 1.5 10^ 10 M_ as a lower limit to its halo mass. No published estimate of stellar halo mass exists for Cen A; we adopt the metallicity along the minor axis at 30 kpc from rejkuba14 .
Three galaxies with classical bulges (M31, NGC 3115, Cen A) indeed have massive metal-rich stellar halos --- carrying >20 of the total galaxy stellar mass --- indicative of a minor or major merger.

Three out of five local galaxies (M31, NGC 3115 and Cen A) with massive classical bulges have massive stellar halos M_ halo > 2 10^ 10 M_ consistent with (but not requiring) a star-rich minor or major merger origin.

sources.summary: The MAGNUM paper describes Centaurus A as a nearby Seyfert 2 early-type galaxy with a dust-lane gas disc and a double-sided radio/X-ray jet linked to ionized halo gas.

quotes.quote: Centaurus A (Seyfert 2 - D _ L 3.82 Mpc - 1" 18.5 pc) is an early-type galaxy characterised by a major axis stellar component, a minor axis dust-lane, and a gas disc. The last follows the dust-lane and comprises the majority of the observed ionised, atomic, neutral, and molecular gas morganti2010 . It is characterised by the presence of a spectacular double-sided radio jet revealed in both the radio and X-rays (e.g. blanco1975,hardcastle2003 ), which was found to match a diffuse highly ionised halo of kraft2008 first identified by bland2003 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Cen A as a major nearby radio-galaxy contributor to UHECR anisotropy, noting that weak boosting would pull anisotropy toward it and that an Auger excess aligns closely with it.

quotes.quote: This figure shows that besides the ``usual suspects'' Cen A (310,+19) , Vir A (284,-74) , For A (240,-57) and Cyg A (76,+6) , for the case of canonical boosting also Per A (151,-13) , 3C 111 (162,-9) , PKS 0521-36 (241,-33) and PKS 1814-63 (331,-21) are expected to contribute to the UHECR flux on a similar level.

We note, however, that this agreement stands and falls with the validity of the ``canonical boosting'' argument -- if ejected cosmic rays from the core jet are fanned out by magnetic fields in the lobes, boosting will be much weaker and ultimately all anisotropy will be drawn towards the direction of Cen A -- the purple crosses mark the positions of the dipoles is boosting is disregarded altogether.
